fun_facts.fact: The production team scouted over 30 villas across Tuscany before settling on a 17th-century estate near Siena, chosen specifically because its overgrown garden gave it a believable 'forgotten' look.
To prepare for her role, the lead actress spent two weeks in Florence taking Italian cooking and language classes — her genuine fumbling with the language made it into the final cut.
The director insisted on shooting the villa's interior scenes with only natural light and practical lamps, giving the film a warm, sun-drenched aesthetic without a single studio lighting rig.
The film's costume designer sourced every piece of the lead character's wardrobe from local Tuscan markets and vintage shops, giving each outfit a lived-in, authentic Italian feel.
Three different actors were offered the male lead before the role was cast; the chemistry read between the final two leads was reportedly so electric it convinced the director in under five minutes.

The screenplay went through 11 drafts over four years, with the writers making a research trip to Tuscany specifically to rewrite the dialogue so it reflected how locals actually speak about love and food.

first_act_hint: The film opens with a breezy, slightly chaotic energy — think sun-soaked roads, a suitcase that won't close, and one very impulsive decision made in the middle of the Italian countryside. Before any romance kicks in, you'll be dropped into a world of gorgeous scenery and delightful awkwardness, so settle in and enjoy the comedy of errors as our heroine gets herself into a situation that's equal parts charming and completely over her head.
